comment_content: The part about looking up and seeing blue sky is inaccurate though. Tornadoes are not like hurricanes. The funnel is about 10% that we see coming down from the cloud base of the supercell. The supercell is anvil shaped because as the storm and updraft strengthen it pushes up against a layer of cold air in the atmosphere called the inversion. Eventually the updraft bursts through that inversion and explodes upwards and strong upper level winds spread it out like the top of an anvil.  There is no hole that opens at the top of the storm.  So depictions of being able to look up into the funnel and see sky (like in the movie Twister and Into the Storm) are not realistic.
reply_content: Searched for a comment that addresses this. Thanks for explaining it so well. The "blue sky" thing is a totally unnecessary dramatization of events by the makers of this film. So much so that I'm now reluctant to watch it.

comment_content: While i understand that the people who lived through this storm likely suffer from PTSD of sorts, documentaries are important.  Preserving these stories is important.  There are documentaries about pretty much ever devastating event in history, and this one should be no different. Teaching about events like these can inspire people to want to learn more about how to prevent this from ever happening again. Even if that's when we're all gone. Just seeing this documentary could inspire that to happen.
reply_content: Can't really prevent a mile long work of God.... Most of us locals are extremely hurt due to the anniversary coming up in general without the help of the documentary... People finally talk about it less and now this documentary is going to open fresh wounds for almost all of us who live here.... This could cause unrepairable damage so some residents mental health as many of the locals are addicts or past addicts, or people that have lost family and friends could initiate the grief all over again, it could reignite many people's PTSD after finally recovering ever so slightly.... I do feel like the rest of the world will see this as educational, Terrifying, maybe even amazing.... But here at home it was devastating, Family surrendering, memory mutilating, absolutely horrible... Nobody will understand how hard it truly was documentary or not... Can't just understand the memory of seeing a Ruined baby nursery, or kids toys tattered, or the terrified screams of survivors, or the actual visualization of the houses you've known your whole life being swallowed with the families you grew up with inside, or praying with your family in the closet because you don't know if you're next....

comment_content:
I hope they mention the butterfly people. My whole family were affected by the storm. I was in Texas at the time but drove down the very next day, I will never forget how scary it was. It looked like a war zone. It was odd not to know where anything was. Somehow the path of the tornado stopped short of my childhood home about four blocks away and took a ninety degree turn away. My cousin and his wife’s home were not so lucky,  their home was completely destroyed. But the countless stories of children seeing butterfly people are incredible and I hope they included this.
We currently live right where the path went through in a home that was recently constructed. I miss the trees that lined this street the most. They were old and glorious. 
Rip to all the peoples whose lives were taken to soon. And whose lives were permanently shaken. Those who still bare the marks physically, emotionally and financially. The life in the city still has a certain cloud. Although much has changed for the better, this grave event still haunts and lingers.

comment_content:
I lived in 20th and Connecticut Ave
Joplin, MO  64801
United States 
Called Connecticut Point! Went through the eye. The tornado sirens went off and suddenly just cut off along with our power. comment_content: They literally ignored the sirens.  🚨
reply_content: We get sirens a couple times a week around here. You get to where you don't feel danger from them after so long with no Danger. It begins to feel like an unnecessary hassle to pack up all your kids and all your animals and go climb down a dank set of steps into a musty, dark, cold cellar. The cellar echoes the sounds of all the lightning and thunder, terrifying your kids. Most of the time, the sirens go off for radar indicated rotation... Meaning there's not a tornado on the ground but we get the sirens anyway. Nobody expected it to be as big as it was or that it was even on the ground. Some people thought it was better to stay home... We have been taught that if you don't have a storm cellar, you should seek an interior room with no windows during a tornado, But that doesn't help you when it sucks the roof off your house and destroys every wall. Sirens are great, but we need to know more about storms to better predict them. I watched a tornado put a stick through a car door. You can't always outrun them. Going underground is best, but not everyone has that option. I am one of three houses on my block with a storm cellar. I live 30 minutes from where this tornado hit.
